planting.note: Sow direct in spring in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; crown vetch spreads aggressively by rhizomes and self-seeding — excellent for erosion control on steep slopes but invasive in natural areas in some states.

directions: Sow direct in spring in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; scarify seeds by nicking or soaking overnight to improve germination, and inoculate with Rhizobium leguminosarum for maximum nitrogen fixation. Coronilla varia is a European leguminous perennial widely planted in North America for highway and railroad bank erosion control due to its vigorous spreading habit, nitrogen fixation, and drought tolerance; it produces pinnate leaves on sprawling, vining stems and clusters of pink-to-white, pea-like flowers from late spring through summer. It is highly invasive in natural areas in much of the US and should NOT be planted near natural habitats; it is best restricted to permanently disturbed, high-maintenance sites such as highway cuts and steep slopes where its aggressive spreading is an asset. Fixes 75–150 lb N/acre when well-established.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral, calcareous soil in full sun; kidney vetch is a key native European wildflower meadow plant and the sole larval food plant of the small blue butterfly.

directions: Sow direct in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, calcareous soil in full sun; kidney vetch is adapted to thin, chalky, limey soils where it excels as a nitrogen fixer and wildflower meadow component. Anthyllis vulneraria is a European native perennial legume of chalk grasslands, limestone pavements, and coastal cliffs, producing distinctive heads of densely clustered, woolly, tubular flowers in shades of yellow, cream, orange, pink, and red depending on subspecies; it is one of the most ornamentally variable native wildflowers of European calcareous grasslands. It is a key component of chalk and limestone wildflower meadow seed mixtures, fixes nitrogen in poor soils, and is the sole larval food plant of the small blue butterfly (Cupido minimus). Outstanding for establishing species-rich wildflower turf on thin, alkaline soils.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring in very well-drained, sandy, acidic to neutral, nutrient-poor soil in full sun; bird's-foot is one of the few nitrogen-fixing legumes adapted to strongly acidic, nutrient-deficient sandy soils where most legumes fail.

directions: Sow direct in spring in very well-drained, sandy, acidic to neutral (pH 4.5–6.5), nutrient-poor soil in full sun; bird's-foot is a specialist of nutrient-poor, acidic, sandy soils and will not thrive in rich, heavy, or alkaline conditions. Ornithopus perpusillus is a small, delicate European annual legume of heathlands, sandy roadsides, and acid grasslands; it produces pinnate leaves on prostrate stems and tiny, pink-and-white, pea-like flowers followed by distinctive, curved, bird's-foot-shaped seed pods in spring and early summer. As a cover crop, it is invaluable for improving sandy, acid soils where other leguminous cover crops cannot establish; it fixes nitrogen, improves soil organic matter, and can be used as a low-growing, self-seeding living mulch in acid-soil crops such as blueberries and azaleas. Self-seeds freely in favorable sandy, acid conditions.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral, calcareous soil in full sun; horseshoe vetch is a chalk and limestone grassland specialist and the sole larval food plant of the adonis blue and chalk hill blue butterflies.

directions: Sow direct in spring in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral, calcareous (chalky or limey) soil in full sun; horseshoe vetch is specialized for thin, alkaline soils and will not thrive on acidic or heavy soils — it is a chalk grassland plant in the truest sense. Hippocrepis comosa is a low-growing, mat-forming European native perennial legume of chalk and limestone grasslands, producing dense, pinnate foliage and clusters of bright-yellow, pea-like flowers in late spring that attract numerous butterfly species; its distinctive seed pods are shaped like a series of horseshoes connected in a chain, giving rise to the common name. It is the larval food plant of the adonis blue (Polyommatus bellargus) and chalk hill blue (Polyommatus coridon) butterflies and is a key component of chalk grassland restoration seed mixtures; it fixes nitrogen in thin, nutrient-poor, alkaline soils where most other nitrogen-fixers cannot compete.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown shrubs in spring in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; scorpion senna is a useful nitrogen-fixing shrub for dry, alkaline bank stabilization and wildlife habitat in zones 7–10.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown shrubs in spring in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; scorpion senna tolerates poor, rocky soils and drought once established and is excellent for dry bank stabilization and wildlife habitat in warm climates. Coronilla emerus (Hippocrepis emerus) is a deciduous to semi-evergreen European native shrub in the pea family producing wiry, green stems with pinnate leaves and clusters of bright-yellow flowers with bronze markings from spring through summer followed by distinctive, elongated, segmented seed pods resembling a scorpion's tail; it is a useful nitrogen-fixing shrub for poor, dry, alkaline soils in zones 7–10. The flowers attract butterflies and bees; the shrub provides nesting cover for birds; it is naturalizing and somewhat invasive in warm, dry climates where conditions suit it. Useful as a medium-height groundcover for dry, sunny banks and slopes.
